subject: FAR
title: Lease Classification and Disclosure Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: A lease simulation asks whether the contract is really a financing arrangement in disguise. Do not start with management's preferred label. Start with control, economics, payment obligation, and disclosure.
microLearning.examTrap: The exam may give one fact that sounds persuasive, such as no legal title transfer, while other facts point toward finance lease treatment.
microLearning.decisionRule: Identify the lease term, payment present value, purchase option, asset life, and whether the arrangement transfers substantially all economic benefits.
microLearning.memoryHook: LEASE: Life, Economics, Amount, Substance, Explanation.

scenario: A controller gives you a lease agreement, payment schedule, purchase option language, and draft financial statement note. Management wants operating lease treatment because it improves leverage ratios.
exhibits: Lease term covers most of the asset's remaining economic life.
Present value of fixed payments is significant relative to fair value.
There is a purchase option described as economically attractive in an internal memo.
Draft disclosure omits the judgment used to classify the lease.
Debt covenants become tighter if a finance lease is recognized.
tasks: List the evidence you would request or recalculate.
Explain the likely financial statement effect.
hints: Look for economic compulsion, not just legal wording.
Debt covenant pressure is a management-bias indicator.
Disclosure should explain significant judgments, not only final labels.

quickChecks.question: Which evidence is most important before accepting operating lease treatment?
options: PV of lease payments and lease term analysis
quickChecks.answer: PV of lease payments and lease term analysis
quickChecks.explanation: Classification depends on economics and control, not preference or invoice appearance.

quickChecks.question: What is the strongest disclosure concern?
quickChecks.explanation: Users need to understand material judgments and obligations.

modelAnswer: A strong response rejects classification based only on management preference. The candidate should evaluate the lease term, present value of fixed payments, purchase option economics, asset life, and covenant pressure as a management-bias indicator. The response should request or recalculate the PV schedule, inspect the agreement and internal memo, explain balance sheet and income statement effects, and recommend disclosure of significant classification judgments and lease obligations.

subject: FAR
title: Revenue Cutoff and Contract Modification Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: Revenue is not earned just because a sale is desired before year-end. The key question is what was promised, what was delivered, and whether control transferred.
microLearning.examTrap: A signed contract near year-end can distract from undelivered performance obligations.
microLearning.decisionRule: Identify performance obligations, transaction price, variable consideration, transfer of control, and cutoff evidence.

scenario: A software company signs a year-end customer amendment that adds implementation services, a discount, and a right to future upgrades. Sales booked the entire invoice as current-year revenue.
exhibits: The original license was delivered before year-end.
Implementation work starts after year-end.
The customer receives a material right to future upgrades.
The amendment changed pricing and service obligations.
Sales compensation is tied to fourth-quarter revenue.
tasks: Assess whether all revenue should be recognized before year-end.
Recommend audit evidence for cutoff and allocation.
hints: Separate delivery from billing.
Look for future obligations hidden inside amendments.
Sales incentives can affect fraud risk.

quickChecks.question: Which fact most directly challenges full current-year recognition?
quickChecks.explanation: Undelivered obligations may require deferral or allocation.

quickChecks.question: What should be tested for the upgrade right?
options: Whether it is a material right
Whether the sales team likes it
quickChecks.answer: Whether it is a material right
quickChecks.explanation: A material right can be a separate performance obligation.

modelAnswer: A strong response identifies multiple performance obligations and does not accept full revenue recognition solely because an invoice exists. Implementation services and upgrade rights require analysis and possible allocation. The sales compensation plan increases fraud risk. Evidence should include the contract, amendment, delivery records, implementation schedule, customer acceptance, price allocation support, and subsequent activity.

subject: AUD
title: Risk Assessment and Evidence Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: Audit risk assessment is detective work. You connect incentives, unusual numbers, weak controls, and evidence gaps into an audit response.
microLearning.examTrap: The exam may include a control deficiency and ask how it changes substantive testing. Do not ignore the control finding.
microLearning.decisionRule: Identify risk, assertion, control impact, procedure, and evidence quality.

scenario: An audit team is planning work for a client with rapid revenue growth, manual journal entries near year-end, and a new compensation plan tied to EBITDA.
exhibits: Revenue increased 38% while cash collections increased 9%.
Management bonuses depend on EBITDA targets.
Several manual entries were posted after normal close.
Prior-year control deficiency over sales cutoff was not fully remediated.
The CFO says the entries are normal clean-up items but provides limited support.
tasks: Select audit responses for revenue and journal entries.
Explain how control deficiencies affect substantive testing.
hints: Growth without cash can indicate collectability or cutoff risk.
Manual entries after close deserve journal-entry testing.
Unremediated controls affect nature, timing, and extent.

quickChecks.question: Which assertion is most directly affected by year-end sales cutoff issues?
quickChecks.explanation: The facts point to possible premature or unsupported revenue.

quickChecks.question: What is the best first response to unsupported post-close entries?
options: Test journal entries and supporting documents
Ignore because they are after close
quickChecks.answer: Test journal entries and supporting documents
quickChecks.explanation: Unsupported manual entries are a classic fraud-risk area.

modelAnswer: A strong response links incentive pressure, unusual growth, weak collections, manual entries, and unresolved cutoff controls to fraud and significant risk. Procedures should include cutoff testing, journal-entry testing, confirmation or subsequent collection work, control remediation evaluation, and documentation of skepticism.

subject: AUD
title: Going Concern and Subsequent Events Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: Going concern is about whether the entity can keep operating long enough to meet obligations. The auditor evaluates management's plan and whether disclosure is adequate.
microLearning.examTrap: A management plan is not enough if it is unrealistic or unsupported.
microLearning.decisionRule: Identify conditions, evaluate management's plan, inspect support, assess disclosure, determine report impact.

scenario: A manufacturing client lost its largest customer after year-end. Management says a new financing agreement will solve liquidity issues, but the agreement is unsigned.
exhibits: The lost customer represented 42% of annual revenue.
Debt covenants are projected to be breached within six months.
Management's forecast assumes replacement sales within one quarter.
The financing term sheet is nonbinding.
Draft financial statement notes mention uncertainty but not covenant risk.
hints: Unsigned financing is weaker than executed financing.
Subsequent events can affect disclosure and going concern evaluation.
Covenant breach risk should not be buried.

quickChecks.question: Which evidence is weakest?
quickChecks.explanation: A nonbinding term sheet does not prove financing is available.

quickChecks.question: What should the auditor challenge?
options: The page number of the note
quickChecks.explanation: Forecast assumptions are central to going concern support.

modelAnswer: A strong response identifies substantial doubt indicators: loss of a major customer, covenant breach risk, liquidity pressure, and unsupported replacement sales. The auditor should evaluate management's plan using binding financing evidence, subsequent sales, cash forecasts, covenant calculations, and board plans. Disclosure should clearly describe uncertainty and covenant risk; report implications depend on adequacy of disclosure.

subject: REG
title: Entity Tax Consequence Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: Tax-free formation is not automatic. Liabilities, basis, boot, and planned distributions can change the answer.
microLearning.examTrap: The phrase 'contribution to entity' can make candidates assume nonrecognition too quickly.
microLearning.decisionRule: Track basis, liabilities, boot, ownership, and planned distributions.

scenario: A client contributes appreciated property and debt to a new business entity and asks whether the transaction is tax-free.
exhibits: Adjusted basis is lower than fair value.
Liabilities exceed basis for one contributed asset.
The client expects cash distributions within 90 days.
Operating agreement changes allocation of income and debt.
The client wants a one-sentence answer for a bank meeting.
hints: Liabilities can trigger deemed boot or gain.
Planned distributions can change the substance.
Never give a tax-free answer before seeing documents.

quickChecks.question: What is the biggest risk when liabilities exceed basis?
quickChecks.explanation: Liability treatment can create gain depending on entity type and facts.

quickChecks.question: What document is essential?
quickChecks.explanation: Allocations, ownership, and debt arrangements affect the tax analysis.

modelAnswer: A strong response does not promise tax-free treatment without analyzing basis, liabilities, deemed distributions, and planned cash movement. It requests contribution agreements, debt documents, valuations, and operating agreements, then explains gain risk and planning alternatives.

subject: ISC
title: Access Control and SOC Readiness Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: Access controls prove that only the right people can do the right things at the right time. SOC readiness depends on evidence, not intentions.
microLearning.examTrap: A completed checklist is not enough if exceptions are not tracked or resolved.
microLearning.decisionRule: Evaluate design, operating effectiveness, exception handling, evidence, and remediation.

scenario: A SaaS company preparing for a SOC engagement has weak user-access reviews, delayed termination removal, and undocumented emergency changes.
exhibits: Quarterly access reviews are marked complete but exceptions are not tracked.
Two terminated employees retained access for more than 20 days.
Emergency changes bypassed approval during an outage.
Management has no evidence of retrospective change review.
The customer success team shares an admin account during busy weeks.
hints: Shared admin accounts weaken accountability.
Operating effectiveness requires evidence across the period.
Emergency change exceptions need retrospective review.

quickChecks.question: Which fact most directly weakens accountability?
quickChecks.explanation: Shared privileged access makes it hard to identify who performed actions.

quickChecks.question: What is missing for emergency changes?
quickChecks.explanation: Emergency changes may be allowed, but they require documented follow-up.

modelAnswer: A strong response identifies access review, termination, emergency change-management, and shared-account gaps. It explains that control design and operating effectiveness may be affected, then recommends exception tracking, timely deprovisioning, retrospective approval, named admin accounts, evidence retention, and management review.

subject: TCP
title: High-Income Individual Planning Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: Planning is not just finding deductions. It is timing income, documenting positions, estimating payments, and avoiding surprise penalties.
microLearning.examTrap: A charitable idea can be good but still fail without valuation, timing, and substantiation.
microLearning.decisionRule: Identify income timing, character, deduction limits, estimated payments, and documentation.

scenario: A high-income taxpayer has equity compensation, charitable intent, state tax exposure, and a pending business sale.
exhibits: Restricted stock vested during the year.
The taxpayer wants to donate appreciated securities.
The business sale may close before year-end.
Estimated payments were based on prior-year income.
The taxpayer moved between two states during the year.
hints: Equity compensation can create withholding gaps.
State residency changes affect allocation.
Donations of appreciated securities need substantiation.

quickChecks.question: Why are estimated payments risky here?
options: Current-year income may be much higher
The taxpayer has a nice portfolio
The sale has a closing binder
quickChecks.answer: Current-year income may be much higher
quickChecks.explanation: Prior-year estimates may underpay when current-year income spikes.

quickChecks.question: What is needed for appreciated securities planning?
quickChecks.explanation: Charitable deductions require substantiation and valuation support.

modelAnswer: A strong response addresses timing, character, estimated tax exposure, charitable donation planning, state allocation, and sale-related income. It requests vesting documents, brokerage records, valuation support, sale agreements, residency information, and prior estimates, then creates an action plan.

subject: BAR
title: Budget Variance and CFO Brief Simulation

microLearning.plainEnglish: A variance simulation asks you to tell the business story behind the numbers. More sales do not always mean better margin.
microLearning.examTrap: Candidates often stop at volume increase and miss price, mix, cost, and efficiency effects.
microLearning.decisionRule: Separate volume, price, cost, mix, efficiency, and one-time items.

scenario: A CFO asks why operating margin fell even though sales volume increased. You have budget, actual, pricing, labor, and materials information.
exhibits: Sales volume exceeded budget by 12%.
Average selling price fell by 5%.
Material costs rose due to supplier changes.
Labor overtime increased because of production rework.
Warranty claims rose for one product line.
tasks: Separate price, volume, cost, and efficiency factors.
hints: Margin can fall when price and cost effects overwhelm volume.
Rework can signal quality or process problems.
CFO communication should be concise and decision-focused.

quickChecks.question: Why can margin fall despite higher volume?
options: Lower price and higher costs offset volume
quickChecks.answer: Lower price and higher costs offset volume
quickChecks.explanation: Volume is only one driver of margin.

quickChecks.question: Which follow-up is most useful?
quickChecks.explanation: Cost and quality issues explain the margin decline.

modelAnswer: A strong response separates volume gains from price pressure, material cost increases, labor inefficiency, and warranty quality issues. It explains margin compression in CFO language and recommends supplier analysis, rework root-cause review, warranty trend review, and price strategy evaluation.
